name: Secondary side entrance
description: A plain service entry on a side or back wall of the building, providing direct access from the exterior to a utility space such as the kitchen, pantry, or scullery. The opening is a single narrow door, typically one block wide and two blocks tall, with no pediment, no flanking pilasters, and no raised threshold step — the floor of the room continues flush with the ground outside so deliveries, firewood, and laundry can be carried in and out without obstruction. Often realised as a Dutch-door or a plain plank door in rustic and medieval contexts, and as an unornamented panel door in Victorian service wings. Ornament is deliberately minimal so the entrance reads as secondary to the building's main door.
